findings.description: Placeholder text 'TBD' remains in the document.

excerpt.text: ptime each calendar month, measured by [TBD methodology].

findings.explanation: Template placeholders such as [insert] or TBD are draft markers that should be filled before signing. Leaving them in is a serious drafting defect.
findings.recommendation: Replace every placeholder with the intended value, or delete the clause.
findings.title: Unfilled template placeholder

findings.description: Found 1 placeholder that looks like unfilled template content: "[TBD methodology]".
findings.explanation: A bracketed placeholder, mustache token, or XXX run that survived into the final document is almost always a drafting accident — the contract is meant to be filled in. A placeholder where a counterparty name belongs makes the agreement legally questionable; a placeholder elsewhere makes the agreement embarrassing.
findings.recommendation: Replace every flagged placeholder with the intended content, or remove the bracket if the content is no longer needed.
findings.title: Unfilled template placeholders: 1

findings.description: The contract specifies a late-payment rate of 2.5% per month, which annualizes to approximately 30.0%.

excerpt.text: fee of $50,000.00 due Net 30. Late fee: 2.5% per month.

findings.explanation: Most US states cap consumer interest in the 18–24%/year range; commercial transactions allow higher contractual rates in many states but several (New York General Obligations Law §5-501, Tennessee, Arkansas pre-Constitutional-Amendment) hold the line tighter. A rate that would be standard in Texas or Delaware may be void in New York or California consumer contexts. Confirm against the controlling jurisdiction.
findings.recommendation: Confirm the rate is enforceable under the controlling state's usury rules. For consumer-facing contracts, consider capping at 18% annualized. For commercial contracts, document the parties' acknowledgement of the rate.

source_citations.source: New York General Obligations Law § 5-501 (civil usury)

findings.title: Late-payment rate above 18%/year: ~30.0%

findings.description: The contract does not allocate ownership of intellectual property.
findings.explanation: Without an IP-ownership clause, default copyright and patent rules apply: under 17 U.S.C. § 201, copyright vests in the author/employee unless work-for-hire or assignment applies.
findings.title: No IP-ownership clause detected

findings.description: The contract references data handling (`Customer Data`) but no clause specifies retention duration or deletion obligations.
findings.explanation: Under GDPR Article 5(1)(e), CCPA, and most modern data-protection regimes, the duration for which data is retained must be defined or tied to a defined purpose. A contract that handles data but says nothing about retention leaves the data subject without a contractual ceiling on how long their information can be held — and leaves the data controller without a clear obligation to delete.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit retention clause: how long data is kept (or the trigger that ends retention), what happens at the end of retention (return / deletion / certification of destruction), and the cooperation timeline.
findings.title: Data retention period unspecified

findings.description: Indemnity sentence counts by party: vendor co=2, customer inc=0, vince vendor=0, caryn customer=0.

excerpt.text: Provider shall indemnify, defend, and hold Customer harmless from any third-party claim that the Service infringes a US patent, copyright, or trade secret.

findings.explanation: One party appears to bear materially more indemnity scope than the other. Confirm the asymmetry is intentional and reciprocated by other consideration (e.g., a fee discount).
findings.title: Indemnity appears asymmetric

findings.description: The limitation-of-liability clause appears to except indemnification, potentially creating uncapped indemnity exposure.

excerpt.text: Provider's aggregate liability shall not exceed three (3) months of fees paid, except for indemnification obligations.

findings.explanation: When indemnity is carved out of the LoL cap, the indemnifying party may face uncapped exposure on third-party claims. Verify a separate indemnity-specific cap exists, or that the carve-out is intended.
findings.title: Indemnity carved out of the liability cap

findings.description: Indemnification language is present (`shall indemnify`) and the liability cap explicitly carves it out — indemnity exposure is uncapped.
findings.explanation: An indemnity carved out of (or simply not subject to) the liability cap can be the largest single financial risk a contract carries. A third-party IP infringement claim, a data-breach notification cost, or a regulatory fine can dwarf the contract value many times over. Confirm the carve-out is deliberate and proportionate to the indemnifying party's solvency.
findings.recommendation: Either subject indemnification to a per-claim and aggregate cap, or confirm the carve-out is a deliberate trade for higher pricing / different insurance.
findings.title: Indemnification carved out of liability cap

findings.description: No Effective Date is named, defined, or stated near the top of this Agreement.
findings.explanation: Most contracts identify a starting point that other date references rely on. Without it, relative terms like 'within 30 days after the Effective Date' have no anchor.
findings.recommendation: Add an Effective Date — either in the preamble ('dated as of [date]'), as a defined term, or as an explicit 'Effective Date: [date]' line.
findings.title: No Effective Date found

findings.description: The contract does not state a path to terminate for material breach.
findings.explanation: Without a for-cause termination path, parties must rely on common-law material-breach doctrines, which are jurisdiction-dependent.
findings.title: No termination-for-cause clause detected

findings.description: The contract does not state what happens upon termination.
findings.explanation: An effect-of-termination clause spells out the rights and obligations that survive, the return or destruction of materials, and the wind-down rules.
findings.title: No effect-of-termination clause detected

findings.description: The document does not state where disputes must be brought.
findings.explanation: Without a venue clause, default venue rules apply (in federal court, 28 U.S.C. § 1391). A clear forum-selection clause avoids ambiguity.
findings.title: No venue / forum clause detected

findings.description: The contract references data but does not separately allocate data ownership.

excerpt.text: Customer Data uploaded to the Service is processed under the DPA. The Service is provided AS-IS.

findings.explanation: IP and data ownership are different. For SaaS, the customer typically owns its data; the vendor typically owns usage analytics. Address each explicitly.
findings.title: Data ownership not separately addressed

findings.description: Only provider bears this typically-mutual obligation.
findings.explanation: Obligations like confidentiality, indemnity, and representations are usually mutual. A one-sided version is sometimes intentional but worth confirming.
findings.title: Asymmetric indemnification obligation

findings.description: The contract uses 'best efforts' rather than 'reasonable efforts' or 'commercially reasonable efforts'.

excerpt.text: Provider shall use best efforts to deliver the Service with 99.99% uptime each calendar month, measured by [TBD methodology].

findings.explanation: Courts in different US jurisdictions interpret 'best efforts' differently — some treat it as an extraordinary standard, others as a synonym for reasonable efforts. 'Commercially reasonable efforts' is generally clearer.
findings.title: 'Best efforts' standard used

findings.description: Provider's aggregate liability shall not exceed three (3) months of fees paid, except for indemnification obligations.

excerpt.text: Provider's aggregate liability shall not exceed three (3) months of fees paid, except for indemnification obligations.

findings.explanation: Negative covenants restrict what a party may do. Surfacing them collectively makes it easier to check they are intended and consistent with the overall deal.
findings.title: Negative covenants: 1

findings.description: The contract uses "best efforts" without defining it.

excerpt.text: Provider shall use best efforts to deliver the Service with 99.99% uptime each calendar month, measured by [TBD methodology].

findings.explanation: Efforts-standard phrases carry different obligation strengths in commercial drafting. *Bloor v. Falstaff* (2d Cir. 1979) and its progeny treat `best efforts` as the most demanding, `commercially reasonable efforts` as the middle term, and `reasonable efforts` as the weakest — but without an in-document definition, the parties can dispute which standard applies and what conduct satisfies it.
findings.recommendation: Either delete the qualifier (let the obligation be absolute) or add an explicit definition of "best efforts" — typically a list of required actions ("including obtaining all consents, devoting professional staff, and absorbing reasonable costs") plus carve-outs ("but not requiring litigation, financial harm, etc.").
findings.title: Efforts standard "best efforts" undefined

findings.description: Indemnity clause appears to be missing: notice, defense control, settlement consent.

excerpt.text: Provider shall indemnify, defend, and hold Customer harmless from any third-party claim that the Service infringes a US patent, copyright, or trade secret.

findings.explanation: A complete indemnity clause specifies (a) the timeline and form for notice of a claim, (b) which party controls defense, and (c) whether settlement requires consent.
findings.title: Indemnity procedural elements missing: notice, defense control, settlement consent

playbook_match_reasoning: Selected saas-customer (score 1). Title matched: "subscription agreement", "master subscription". Required clauses present: "indemnification". Distinguishing phrases: "the Service", "Subscription Term", "Customer Data", "uptime".
